slide_1_Cover: Industrial AI Deployment Failure Diagnosis & Recovery
slide_2_Idea_Problem_Solution_Value: Time-to-diagnosis cut from 3-6 months to 2 weeks, minimizing production downtime and enabling early operational recovery for stalled AI investments
Remediation costs reduced from $70K+ to $7K-$21K, making recovery accessible to small and mid-size manufacturers who cannot afford Big 4 consulting fees
Redeployment success rate raised from below 40% to above 80%, delivering measurable ROI on the factory's existing AI investment at fraction of rework cost
On-site diagnosis identifies root causes within 2 weeks, replacing the 3-6 month remediation cycle with a structured root-cause map and actionable recovery plan
5-professor academic network and 700+ B2B network in Daegu-Gyeongbuk enable simultaneous technical validation and government-channel customer acquisition
Recovery cases build a proprietary failure database that categorizes patterns by industry and vendor, enabling algorithmic factory-vendor matching over time
80% of industrial AI deployments fail post-contract per RAND 2024 and Gartner 2025, leaving $70K-$700K in idle equipment and months of lost production
Internal rework, vendor renegotiation, and Big 4 consulting cannot diagnose cross-domain root causes — remediation costs can reach up to 100x the original investment
No specialized recovery service exists for Korea's 30,000+ smart factories, forcing them to write off losses or abandon failed AI projects entirely
slide_3_Market_1_3_TAM_SAM_SOM_+_Why_Now: 30,000+ smart factories with highest AI adoption density in Asia — 700+ B2B network and government channels pre-secured in Daegu-Gyeongbuk region
MSS budget surged 85% YoY to $310M in 2026 driving AI adoption — yet Gartner confirms only 28% of deployments fully succeed creating structural rescue demand
RAND 2024 and Gartner 2025 confirm AI deployment failure is global — expansion path runs Korea to Korean-owned US plants then Japan and SEA markets
Korea's manufacturing AI budget is surging — government alone commits $450M+ in 2026
SMB and mid-market factories actively deploying AI
MSS Smart Factory expansion ($328M, +85% YoY) and AX integrated portfolio ($318M) anchor public spend — private manufacturing AI adds $245M at 52% CAGR through 2030
Korea Inc.'s $150B US plant commitment (semiconductors, batteries, EVs) unlocks $1–2B/yr additional AI-deployment market from 2027
30–40% of Korea TAM sits in SMB/mid-market AI deployment projects — MSS supplier approval unlocks direct government-channel access
Excludes KRW 1B+ enterprise deals dominated by incumbents and large-corp internal R&D — this is the addressable gap

Current $27K/week revenue run-rate validates the 3-year Korea SOM. 5-year expansion layers in Platform SaaS ($2.3–3.8M), US Phase 3 Korean plant deployments ($2.3–3.8M), and government-channel concentrated deals ($2.3–3.8M) — totaling $11–19M annual SOM by year 5
slide_4_Market_2_3_Competition: Manufacturing AX and MLOps leader with IPO review passed — strongest productized Korean incumbent setting the category standard
Machine vision and smart factory inspection with deep product line — closest Korean competitor in camera-heavy deployment space
Andrew Ng's vision AI platform with productized deployment tools — stronger product but lacks Korean on-site delivery density
Industrial AI with diagnosis-to-recurring revenue at scale — validates the model but targets machine health not deployment rescue
Zero direct competitors in fix-failed-AI position — rescue cases compound into a failure pattern database that strengthens the first-mover moat over time
5-professor academic network at KNU plus 700+ regional B2B network provide simultaneous technical depth and customer access that productized competitors lack
Government certifications with 40-50% of customers acquired via public programs at zero CAC — incumbents compete on product while entry is through trust and access
slide_5_Market_3_3_GTM_Pricing_BM_Scale: Project-based Rescue fees then vendor matching commissions then platform SaaS subscription as 3-stage revenue model
3 paying customers and $25K revenue in Week 1, validating the revenue model immediately post-pivot
Phase 1: Leverage 700+ B2B network and government channels in Daegu-Gyeongbuk to deliver Rescue diagnosis services to factories with failed AI deployments
Phase 2: After 50+ rescue cases, transition to failure-pattern-DB-powered factory-vendor matching platform as Solutions Architect with deployment QA
Phase 3: Expand to Korean-owned US plants via Plug and Play Sunnyvale, then scale to Japan, SEA, and global markets as a platform SaaS model
Starting with 100% expert-led delivery, targeting 2-week to 1-week diagnosis at 50 cases and margin improvement from 50% to 65% through structural efficiency
At 100+ accumulated failure cases algorithmic factory-vendor matching becomes viable reaching the technical tipping point for platform transition
Government channels via InnoBiz, MainBiz, Venture certifications enable 40-50% of customers at zero CAC supporting profitability-driven scaling
slide_6_Team_1_4_Composition_&_Roles: CEO Jiho spent 5 years selling and deploying industrial AI, building a 700+ B2B network and experiencing deployment failure patterns firsthand — uniquely positioned for rescue GTM

CTO Minho trained at Kyungpook National University KNUVI lab bridging a 5-professor academic network while leading the corporate R&D institute — combines on-site technical diagnosis with research depth
Pre-formed team with 10+ joint project history and $25K Week 1 revenue post-pivot — complementary CEO sales and CTO technical skills with demonstrated immediate product-market fit
Kyungpook National University Computer Science; founded Bigtablet 2021, 6yr operating experience
5yr direct industrial AI sales; deployment failure patterns known firsthand
$210K personal capital plus $350K govt financing committed; fully invested
Kyungpook National University MS Computer Science; IEEE and MPEG standardization experience
10yr vision AI field deployment with deep accumulated domain expertise
Leads GPU/CUDA/STT pipeline dev and heads the corporate R&D institute
slide_7_Team_2_4_Program_Trajectory: Jiho and Minho co-founded Bigtablet in 2021 and joined Antler KOR8 as a pre-formed team with 10+ joint project history and complementary sales plus technical roles
Started as 3-person team with Mohsen Mirali in Week 1-2 then restructured to Jiho-Minho core duo — team realigned around industrial AI deployment rescue thesis
CEO handles sales and pitch while CTO covers technology and product — team alignment strengthened through program iterations filling each other's domain gaps
Received Red signals in Bootcamps 1-3 with feedback to clarify customer definition and sharpen core value proposition — persistent iteration through negative feedback
Pivoted to industrial AI on-site diagnosis in Bootcamp 4 converting from Red to Green — feedback emphasized clearer communication of core product beyond consulting
Tracked out in Bootcamp 5 with Gabriel Jung as sponsor partner — feedback highlighted the need for innovative software positioning beyond pure consulting service
Post-trackout: crystallized core as rescue diagnosis with paying customers — $25K Week 1 revenue validating the pivot toward industrial AI failure recovery service
Summarizes the journey from program start through team formation and PMF validation, based on weekly founder retrospectives and program team observations
slide_8_Team_3_4_Evaluation: Pre-formed team, clear roles, aligned values. Work-intensity gap: 70h vs 40h/wk expectation
10+ AI projects delivered, $350K govt financing. Red to Green pivot during program. Strong execution
3 Red signals BC1-3, improved each round via feedback. Prior co-founder conflict exp gives crisis mgmt
Deep customer understanding of failed AI factories. Week 1 revenue validates demand. TAM/SAM needs work
VCM, vision AI, CUDA/GPU, STT pipeline. IEEE research plus MPEG standards exp. KG prototype early-stage
Domain KG for hidden variables from transcripts is differentiated. Must prove innovation vs consulting
Based on weekly founder retrospectives and feedback
slide_9_Team_4_4_Strengths_&_Improvement: Strengths
● Problem backed by Gartner and RAND data
Only 28% of industrial AI deployments fully succeed per Gartner — structural rescue pain is real and verified by global sources
● Camera-vision expertise fits rescue wedge
VCM and vision AI expertise directly maps to camera-heavy deployment failures — the most common industrial AI failure pattern
● Week 1 revenue validates rescue demand
Revenue from 3 engagements in first week post-pivot — demand signal strong but customer and revenue definitions need confirmation
● Regional network enables zero-CAC entry
700+ B2B network plus InnoBiz and MainBiz certifications drive initial customers through government channels at near-zero CAC
Areas for Improvement
● Public narrative mismatches investor story
Website still shows video analytics and retail — rescue positioning exists only in investor materials not in public identity
● Service not platform at current stage
All revenue comes from founder-led rescue delivery — Failure DB and matching engine are hypotheses not products at this stage
● Traction definition internally inconsistent
3 paying engagements alongside Has Customers No in same memo — revenue recognition and customer definition need clarity
● IP ownership chain involves university risk
KNU technology transfer raises IP assignment risk — faculty and student invention ownership needs legal confirmation before invest
● Founder dependence limits scaling path
Diagnoses depend on CEO network and CTO technical judgment — no repeatable delivery framework exists beyond founder-led execution
Team (4/4)
Summary
● Pre-Antler, Jiho holds 100%; at Antler closing, the cap table shifts to 85% (Jiho), 10% (CTO via secondary purchase), and 5% ESOP,
reflecting solo founding contribution, aligned incentives, and planned expansion of the option pool post-seed.
Founder Alignment & Equity Structure
slide_10_Investor_Assessment: Not big enough if confined to vision AI field. The team is not showing clearly how to expand into other AI fields
No direct competition observable due to complexity of the business and the required expertise to enter the market
The team's expertise, network access, execution capacity are all high setting barriers against competition
Fast real revenue coming from SMBs and factories

Converting factories into smart factories will require similar elements and optimization processes. So, what's obtained from Korean factories are transferrable to other markets such as Vietnam. Not quite sure about US where smart factories are being setup ground-up

Strong CEO with domain expertise and sales execution capabilities. Good CTO with Vision AI expertise only but not quite excellent in SW and systems engineering - will probably need one soon. Team of 9 ppl in total - short runway

Overall Summary : Strong team with domain expertise, good business idea in the right market at the right timing. Although starting as an agency business, fast evolution into scalable business can be expected. Good early traction immediately after pivoting into this idea

Key Risks : If evolving into a non-agency business gets late, the team will suffer from low-margin operation due to labor-intensiveness of the initial form of the service. If the business focus is confined to vision AI solutions only, the team's growth rate will be limited
